coredata.dc:title: Heating-free synthesis of red emissive carbon dots through separated processes of polymerization and carbonization
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Hongcan
Xia, Pengfei
Shao, Haibao
Zhang, Rong
Lu, Changgui
Xu, Shuhong
Wang, Chunlei
coredata.dc:description: Polymerization and carbonization are believed as two basic processes for the bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots (CDs). Since these two processes usually occur simultaneously due to the high reaction temperature and fast reaction rate, it is still a challenge to separate and control these two processes. In the present work, we reported a new room temperature method, which achieved the separated and controlled polymerization and carbonization processes. The polymerization process is realized by dissolving o-phenylenediamine (OPD) in ethanol at room temperature, and finally obtained polymer dots (PDs) without any lattice with a sphere size of 29.6 nm. The carbonization process begins in a manual way by adding concentrated sulfuric acid. After carbonization, CDs (noted as CPDs in this work) with a size of 3.6 nm and a clear lattice can be obtained. Importantly, the separated polymerization and carbonization make us possible to adjust the composition or interactions of intermediate products during the synthesis process. As a prototype, we added acetic acid (AA) additives into OPD precursors during the polymerization stage. Due to the crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) effect via hydrogen bonds which are produced by the amide groups from AA reaction products with H in the –NH3 + or aromatic ring, the resulted CPDs show improved PLQY from an initial 6.87% (without AA) to 16.47%. The current work realized the separated and controllable polymerization and carbonization processes, opening up the door for tuning the composition and interactions of intermediate products before carbonization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emissive carbon dots
Separation processes of polymerization
Carbonization
Fluorescence regulation
Heating-free synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CPDs with the introduction of AA additives in the polymerization process
The separated polymerization and carbonization processes during CPDs preparation
Fluorescence origin of PDs and CPDs
Improved PLQY of CPDs by regulating the polymerization process

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22075043, 21875034, 12274075 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) HRTEM image of CPDs; (c)TGA and DTG, (d) XRD spectrum and (e) XPS survey spectrum of CPDs; (f) High-resolution N1s spectra of CPDs; (h) Schematic illustration of the preparation of CPDs.

PL and PLE spectra of PDs (a) and CPDs (b); (c) Absorption spectra of PDs and CPDs; (d) Absorption spectra of CPDs (black curve) and fluorescent molecules (red curve), respectively; (e) PL spectra of CPDs (black curve) and fluorescent molecules (red curve), respectively. As-prepared CPDs was purified by dialysis membrane. Then, the fluorescent molecular powder was obtained by freeze-drying the aqueous solution which was outside the dialysis membrane. (f) TGA and DTG curves of PDs treated with dilute sulfuric acid at pH = 1; PL spectra of PDs treated with hydrochloric acid (g) and nitric acid (h); (i) FTIR spectra of PDs and CPDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

PL and PLE spectra (a) and absorption spectra (b) of CPDs-AA; (c) TGA and DTG curves of CPDs-AA; 1H NMR of CPDs-AA (d) and CPDs (e); (f) PLQY of CPDs at different stages with the introduction of AA additives; (g) Schematic illustration of CPDs-AA formation.
Strategy for the synthesis of CPDs at room temperature.
The relative contents of different compositions of N for PDs and CPDs.

head.ce:title: Heating-free synthesis of red emissive carbon dots through separated processes of polymerization and carbonization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronics & Information, Nantong University, Nantong 226019, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronics & Information, Nantong University, Nantong 226019, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, the Second Hospital of Nanjing, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, the Second Hospital of Nanjing, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A method for the synthesis of CDs at room temperature through separable and manipulable polymerization and carbonization process.

Polymerization and carbonization are believed as two basic processes for the bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots (CDs). Since these two processes usually occur simultaneously due to the high reaction temperature and fast reaction rate, it is still a challenge to separate and control these two processes. In the present work, we reported a new room temperature method, which achieved the separated and controlled polymerization and carbonization processes. The polymerization process is realized by dissolving o-phenylenediamine (OPD) in ethanol at room temperature, and finally obtained polymer dots (PDs) without any lattice with a sphere size of 29.6 nm. The carbonization process begins in a manual way by adding concentrated sulfuric acid. After carbonization, CDs (noted as CPDs in this work) with a size of 3.6 nm and a clear lattice can be obtained. Importantly, the separated polymerization and carbonization make us possible to adjust the composition or interactions of intermediate products during the synthesis process. As a prototype, we added acetic acid (AA) additives into OPD precursors during the polymerization stage. Due to the crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) effect via hydrogen bonds which are produced by the amide groups from AA reaction products with H in the –NH3 + or aromatic ring, the resulted CPDs show improved PLQY from an initial 6.87% (without AA) to 16.47%. The current work realized the separated and controllable polymerization and carbonization processes, opening up the door for tuning the composition and interactions of intermediate products before carbonization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), also known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or carbon nanodots (CNDs), are a new type of zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials that are widely used in bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, and photocatalysis.[1–3] As the most common synthesis method for CDs, the bottom-up method is favorable for preparing CDs with bright fluorescence.[4,5] However, due to the extremely fast reaction process of CDs as well as the lack of effective in-situ monitor technique for the environment of bottom-up synthesis (for instance sealed autoclave at high temperature),[6–8] it is always a challenge to understand the formation mechanism of precursor molecules to CDs. Unlike to nucleation and growth mechanism of inorganic nanocrystals, the formation of CDs is believed more similar to the formation of polymers. By studying the formation mechanism of CDs synthesized using citric acid and ethanolamine, Giannelis's group suggests that precursors are transformed into amorphous polymer dots (PDs) that further evolve into graphitic CDs in a longer reaction time or at a higher reaction temperature.[9] Xiong et al. reported that the formation process of CDs starts with the polymerization of nearby o-phenylenediamine molecules, and as the reaction time increases the carbonization of the intermediate polymer forms CDs through deamination and dehydrogenation reactions.[10] Although some progress has been made in the study of the formation mechanism of CDs, some fundamental challenges still exist. For instance, polymerization and carbonization usually occur in an uncontrollable manner due to the fast reaction rate and high reaction temperature for the most commonly used solvothermal or microwave methods. During the carbonization process, the polymerization process is still going on. If the polymerization and carbonization processes can be separated, just like the separated nucleation and growth processes of some inorganic nanocrystals, it will give us more opportunities to investigate the relationship between the structure and fluorescence of CDs by controlling either the polymerization or the carbonization process. It also opens up the door for tuning the composition and interactions of intermediate products before carbonization. More recently, Fan's group observed the prepared CDs have no lattice at a relatively low reaction temperature of 120℃, but a clear lattice at a relatively high reaction temperature above 210℃.[11] However, the synthesis of CDs proceeded in the sealed autoclave at high temperatures, making it difficult to adjust the composition or interactions of the possible intermediate products during the polymerization or carbonization processes. Obviously, it still needs to develop a proper method not only to realize the separated polymerization and carbonization processes but also to achieve controllable composition or interactions of the intermediate products during the synthesis of CDs.

Recently, room-temperature (RT) synthesis of CDs has attracted more and more attention. Different from most bottom-up methods such as solvothermal and microwave methods which require high reaction temperature or high power, RT synthesis of CDs is more energy-saving and more closing to the requirements of green chemistry synthesis. For example, Zeng et al. reported a one-pot RT synthesis of CDs based on Schiff base reaction cross-linking between triethylenetetramine and methyl-p-benzoquinone.[12] Huang's group synthesized fluorescent CDs using o-phenylenediamine, dopamine, and H2O2 as precursors at room temperature.[13] On the basis of current reference reports, it can be found that the focus of RT synthesis of CDs is mainly on the aspect of energy-saving. Actually, the RT synthesis method can achieve a slow reaction rate at the open reaction vessel. In comparison to the sealed high-temperature autoclave for the solvothermal method, the RT synthesis method maybe provide a unique opportunity for separating the polymerization and carbonization of CDs. In this sense, the RT synthesis method possibly has a more important role in controlling the formation process of CDs besides the current focus aspect of energy-saving.

Herein, we achieved separated polymerization and carbonization via RT synthesis of CDs (noted as CPDs in this work) with o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the sole precursor. The polymerization process is realized by a slow reaction of OPD in ethanol solvent at room temperature, resulting in loose polymer dots (PDs) have a relatively large average size of 29.6 nm without any lattice. The carbonization process can be controlled in a manual way through adding of concentrated sulfuric acid into PDs. After carbonization, the prepared CPDs have a relatively small average size of 3.6 nm with a clear lattice. It should be emphasized that the current RT synthesis provides the opportunity to control polymerization and carbonization processes. As an example, acetic acid (AA) additives were introduced into the OPD precursor solution during the polymerization stage, giving rise to an increased PLQY of 6.87% for the initial CPDs (without AA) to the 16.47% for the final CPDs (with AA). It is found that AA additives promote the formation of amide during the polymerization stage, making strong hydrogen bonds between the polymer chains of CPDs and hence improving PLQY by crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) effect.

O-phenylenediamine (OPD, CP ≥ 98.5%), acetic acid (AA, GR ≥ 99.8%), ethanol (AR ≥ 99.7%), methanol (GR), dichloromethane (AR), ethyl ether (AR), acetone (AR), Toluene (AR), tetrahydrofuran (THF, AR), hydrochloric acid (HCl, AR 36.0～38.0%), nitric acid (HNO3, AR 65.0～68.0%) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, AR 99.5%) and n-hexane (AR, 97%) were purchased from Macklin. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, AR > 99%) and 2,3-diaminophenazine (2,3-DAPN, >90%) were purchased from Aladdin. The 500 Da dialysis bag was obtained from Hefei Bomei Biotechnology Co., Ltd.
In general, a strategy of polymerization and carbonization performed separately was used to synthesize the target CPDs at room temperature.

Typically, 0.324 g of OPD was added to 10 mL of ethanol and then stirred at room temperature for 3 days. Then, the mixed solution was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 500 for 12 h. Finally, the polymer dots (PDs) solutions are dried for further use.

To obtain typical CPDs, 200 mg of PDs was placed in a beaker and 2 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid was added to it. After carbonization for 30 min, the product was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 500 for 12 h. Finally, pure solid CPDs can be obtained by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CPDs with the introduction of AA additives in the polymerization process
ce:section.ce:para: The specific synthesis process was same to that of the CPDs mentioned above. Briefly speaking, 0.324 g of OPD and 0.34 mL of AA additives were added to 10 mL of ethanol and stirred at room temperature for 3 days. Then, pure solid polymer dots (symbolized by PDs-AA) was obtained by dialysis and freeze-drying the mixed solution. Subsequently, 200 mg of PDs-AA was carbonization by concentrated sulfuric acid (2 mL) for 30 min, and purified CPDs (symbolized by CPDs-AA) was obtained after dialysis and freeze-drying treatment.

UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded with the L9 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer produced. Photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation spectra (PLE) measurements were performed with a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC fluorescence spectrometer. The absolute PL quantum yield (PLQY) of CPDs was recorded using an Edinburgh instrument FS5 spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere at an excitation wavelength of 600 nm. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) were measured using FEI Tecnai F20. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Thermos Scientific ESCAlab 250xi spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a vertex 80 V FTIR spectrometer. The FTIR samples were prepared by a tablet containing CPDs and KBr. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured using a Thermos scientific ESCAlab 250xi spectrometer with monochromatized Al Kα as the X-ray source. 1H NMR spectra were performed with an AVANCE III HD 600 MHz spectrometer using deuterated DMSO as the solvent. And tetramethylsilane was recommended as an internal reference compound. The decomposition temperature thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was done using a Thermo Cahn TherMax 500 thermogravimetric analyzer in nitrogen condition from room temperature to 800 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) curve was obtained by the first order derivative on temperature of the TGA curve. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) was performed using Agilent 1260 liquid chromatography·THF has been applied as the eluent with a flow rate of 1 mL/min. And polystyrene standards are used to calibrate. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was measured using Thermos Scientific ESCAlab 250xi spectrometer.

A new RT and heating-free synthesis method of CDs with manipulable polymerization and carbonization processes was developed (Scheme 1 ). Briefly, the process of polymerization was performed by stirring OPD in ethanol at room temperature for 3 days, resulting in an intermediate product of PDs with a yellow emission. Then, the carbonization process was carried out by adding concentrated sulfuric acid to the PDs powder, leading to the final product of CPDs with a red emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The separated polymerization and carbonization processes during CPDs preparation
ce:section.ce:para: Different from most synthesis methods of CDs with the concomitant polymerization and carbonization processes, the current method can achieve independent polymerization and carbonization processes, making it possible to manipulate the formation and emission of CDs at different reaction states.

The carbonization process is realized by treating PDs with concentrated sulfuric acid to form CPDs as confirmed by the following results. (1) From the TEM image (Fig. 1
a and 1b), CPDs demonstrate a dot-like shape with an average diameter of 3.6 nm and a lattice distance of 0.28 nm that is attributed to the (020) plane of graphite.[21,22] The dramatically decreased size and the formation of crystal lattice after sulfuric acid treatment indicate the carbonization of PDs. (2) From TGA and DTG curves (Fig. 1 c), it can be confirmed that there are five weight loss processes in CPDs based on previous research results and the weight loss curves of the OPD in Figure S6 , which correspond to different compositional changes as discussed in detail in Note S1 of the Supporting Information. [23,24] Different from PDs with a single weight loss process, as-prepared CPDs show more weight loss processes. Especially, the degradation at a high temperature above 250 °C was ascribed to decomposition from the carbon core and the polymer chains linked to the carbon core, suggesting CPDs belong to carbonized polymer dots with carbon core and an amorphous polymer shell. [23–26] From XRD results (Fig. 1 d), as-prepared CPDs has a series of sharp diffraction peaks rather than the typical broad peaks around 26°. Similar results are also observed in CPDs synthesized in previous reports. [18,27] (3) From the full XPS spectrum of CPDs in Fig. 1 e, CPDs show similar chemical composition to that of PDs, except for the newly appeared S element.[28] Since free sulfuric acid has been removed during the purification of the sample (see experimental section), the measured signal of S should be the doped S atoms in CPDs through the chemical reaction between PDs and sulfuric acid. In addition, the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 1 f and Table 1 ) show dramatically decreased pyrrole N or amino N but increased graphite N in CPDs than that in PDs. It suggests an enlarged conjugation domain by treatment of concentrated sulfuric acid.

Based on the aforementioned results, the possible formation mechanism for CPDs is shown in Fig. 1 g, the reactant OPD undergoes a condensation process during stirring in ethanol at room temperature, resulting in the formation of polymer chains and PDs by the entangled polymer chains (namely step 1, polymerization process). By adding concentrated sulfuric acid, the PDs are dehydrated and carbonized, resulting in the formation of CPDs that have carbon cores with a lattice and surface wrapped with many polymer chains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence origin of PDs and CPDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence origin of CDs is usually believed from the molecular state, the carbon core state, or the surface state. In this work, the emission of both PDs and CPDs should originate from the molecular state based on the following results. (1) From Fig. 2
a and 2b, PDs show a yellow emission around 560 nm with an absolute PLQY of 2.86% measured by using an integrating sphere, whereas CPDs show a red emission around 620 nm with a PLQY of 6.87%. Since PDs had no carbon core before being treated with concentrated sulfuric acid, the yellow emission of PDs is not from the carbon core state. (2) From absorption spectra in Fig. 3
c, the absorption region before 300 nm (region 1) is corresponding to the π → π* transition.[29,30] And the other broadband above 300 nm (region 2) usually attributed to the n → π* transition of the surface states or the molecular state. [31,32] The PLE spectra in Fig. 2 a-b shows that the target fluorescence peak is generated by excitation at wavelengths above 300 nm. It means that the fluorescence of PDs and CPDs originates from the molecular or surface state rather than the carbon core state. (3) In a control experiment, as-prepared CPDs without any purification treatment were dialyzed for 12 h, then PL and absorption spectra of the solution inside and outside the dialysis bag were obtained respectively. Since the cut-off molecular weight is only 500 Da for the dialysis membrane, only small molecules can penetrate the dialysis membrane, leaving large molecular weight particles inside the dialysis membrane. In this situation, if the fluorescence of PDs or CPDs originates from the molecular state, small fluorescent molecules that are not linked to the carbon dots can penetrate the dialysis membrane, leading to a similar emission for the solution both inside and outside the dialysis membrane, which does not exist if the fluorescence originates from the surface state. According to the results in Fig. 2 d-e and
Figure S7 , the fluorescence and absorption of the solutions inside and outside of the dialysis bag are identical, confirming the emission of both PDs and CPDs from the molecular state.

Actually, it is believed that PDs and CPDs have the same molecular state fluorophore. As shown in the control experiment by varying the pH values of both PDs and CPDs in Figure S8 , PDs and CPDs under acidic conditions (pH 4.0 and 6.0) show almost the same emission peak at 621–623 nm and a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 26–27 nm. Under alkaline conditions (pH 8.0 and 10.0), both PDs and CPDs show similar emission peaks around 560–563 nm and FWHM of 61–62 nm. Since PDs and CPDs in either acidic solutions or alkaline solutions show similar PL spectra, it indicates that PDs and CPDs have the same molecular state fluorophore. According to the reference work of CPDs with sole reactant of OPD,[33] the fluorophore is believed as 2,3-diaminophenazine (2,3-DAPN) in CPDs. Herein, we also compared the PL spectra of PDs with that of commercial 2,3-DAPN ( Figure S9
a) before and after protonation. The results in Figures S9
b and S9c show almost the same emission profile between 2,3-DAPN and PDs no matter before and after protonation (treated by pH 1.0 dilute sulfuric acid). It confirms that the molecular state fluorophores of PDs and CPDs should be 2,3-DAPN.

A striking difference between PDs and CPDs is the red shift of emission peak after carbonization by concentrated sulfuric acid. Since the aforementioned results indicate that the emissions of PDs or CPDs originate from the molecular state rather than the carbon core state, it suggests that the additional effect of concentrated sulfuric acid besides carbonization is to change the original molecular state of the PDs to another new molecular state in CPDs. In the control experiments where diluted sulfuric acid with pH = 1.0 was used to treat PDs, the single DTG peaks below 250 °C in Fig. 2 f confirm no carbon core structure in the sample since the diluted sulfuric acid loses the necessary capacity of strong oxidation and dehydration for the carbonization process. Interestingly, PL spectra for the sample treated with diluted sulfuric acid in Figure S10 exhibit the same red-shifted emission around 620 nm as that sample treated with concentrated sulfuric acid in Fig. 2 b. The second role of concentrated sulfuric acid is independent of the capacity of oxidation and dehydration but relevant to H+ in sulfuric acid. In Fig. 2 g and 2 h, when hydrochloric acid (HCl) and concentrated nitric acid (HNO3) were used for the treatment of PDs, similar red-shifted emission can be also observed. It confirms the effect of H+ in changing the molecular state emission of PDs into that of CPDs. From FTIR results in Fig. 2 i, it can be seen the –NH2 groups on PDs is converted to –NH3 + on CPDs after acid treatment, which should be the reason for the emission red-shift. Similar observations are also reported in previous work, where the protonation of –NH2 groups leads to changed emission from yellow to red fluorescence.[33] It is believed that the conversion from –NH2 groups to –NH3 + groups can alter the charge distribution and increased the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy level according to Figure S11 in the supporting information, ultimately achieving a transformation from the yellow emission at 560 nm to the red emission at 620 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improved PLQY of CPDs by regulating the polymerization process
ce:section.ce:para: Since polymerization and carbonization proceed independently in the current synthesis method, it gives us a unique opportunity to improve the fluorescence of CPDs by modulating these two processes. As an example, we herein demonstrate the effect of AA additives in the polymerization process. The obtained intermediate products after polymerization were named PDs-AA. After treatment with concentrated sulfuric acid, the final product was named CPDs-AA.

As shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b, it is obvious that the fluorescence and absorption spectra profiles of CPDs-AA are identical to those of CPDs. However, the PLQY is improved with AA additives in Figure S12 (6.87% for CPDs and 16.47% for CPDs-AA). The TGA and DTG curves for CPDs-AA in Fig. 3 c indicate that the weight loss processes from 200 to 320 °C and 320 to 490 °C correspond to the decomposition of the polymer chains and the degradation of the cross-linked polymer network, respectively. A total of 58.88% weight loss attributed to polymer chain decomposition was achieved during these two stages. Such high-content polymer chains can provide massive donor and acceptor sites for hydrogen bonds, leading to improved PLQY by the CEE effect.

To provide more evidence, XPS and 1H NMR tests were used to analyze the donor and acceptor groups of the hydrogen bond. (1) The CO groups in amide, which are introduced by the reaction between the –NH2 and AA, are believed as the hydrogen bond acceptor in this work.[34–36] XPS results of CPDs-AA in Figure S13 and S14 show similar types of chemical bonds (CC/CC, CN, CS, CO, and CO) but higher content of CO (47.7% in Table S2 ) in CPDs-AA than that of CPDs (16.5% in Table S2 ). As shown in the 1H NMR results of CPDs-AA in Fig. 3 d, a new peak assigned as amide with a chemical shift of 2.83 ppm appears.[37] It suggests the higher content of CO in CPDs-AA derived from the formation of amide after the introduction of AA. It should be noted that amide formed in the polymerization stage according to the 1H NMR of PDs-AA in Figure S15 . It suggests AA additives can regulate functional groups of PDs at room temperature in the polymerization stage. (2) The H in –NH3 + and H in the benzene ring are identified as hydrogen bond acceptors. As shown in the 1H NMR results of Fig. 3 e, a clear moving of –NH3 + from 6.65 ppm for CPDs to 8.83 ppm for CPDs-AA is detected, indicating the formation of strong hydrogen bonds in CPDs-AA with H of –NH3 + as the hydrogen bond donor.[38,39] Simultaneously, it is obvious that the peak of benzene ring H from CPDs moved to the lower chemical shift in CPDs-AA (7.13 ppm to 7.32 ppm, 7.02 ppm to 7.21 ppm), which means the benzene ring H can also be used as a donor to form hydrogen bonds.[19,33].

On the basis of the above results, it can be deduced that the possible structures of hydrogen bonds in CPDs-AA are CO···HN and CO···H-Ar. the introduction of AA leads to more CO acceptor sites in the polymer chains on the surface of CPDs-AA, and thus more and stronger hydrogen bonds. As a result, free polymer chains could be effectively immobilized. The non-radiative recombination via the rotation and vibration of the molecular state can be restrained and thus achieving a higher PLQY of CPDs-AA (16.47%) than that of CPDs (6.87%). In other words, the method of using AA additives in the polymerization stage can improve the PLQY of CPDs by the CEE effect. [40–42].

Interestingly, the introduction of the AA additives at the carbonization stage rather than the polymerization stage has no effect on the fluorescence properties of the CPDs. In a control experiment, the single precursor OPD was stirred in ethanol for 3 days at room temperature to obtain PDs. Then, the CPDs (symbolized by CPDs-ctrl) were obtained by adding concentrated sulfuric acid and acetic acid simultaneously to the purified and dried PDs powder. As shown in Fig. 3 f, it can be seen that the PLQY of CPDs-ctrl (6.88%) is considerably lower than that of CPDs-AA (16.59%) and close to that of CPDs (7.02%). It seems that AA does not work in this control experiment. The reason is the weak competition of the reaction between AA and –NH2 over the reaction between concentrated sulfuric acid and –NH2. Besides the fast carbonization reaction, concentrated sulfuric acid can also convert –NH2 to –NH3 + which can not react with the carboxyl group as confirmed by Figure S16 . Obviously, these results indicate that the impact of AA is more effective in the polymerization stage rather than the carbonization stage.

In conclusion, this work reports a RT method that achieved a separable polymerization and carbonization process. Moreover, acetic acid (AA) additives were added into OPD precursors during the polymerization stage, which made PLQY of CPDs improve from an initial 6.87% (without AA) to 16.47% due to a crosslinking enhanced emission (CEE) effect via hydrogen bonding. The current work not only provides an opportunity to obtain a deeper understanding of the formation mechanism of CDs, but also provides a more controllable way to regulate the fluorescence properties of CDs. In addition, the synthesis of CDs in this paper was carried out at room temperature without additional energy source. Therefore, energy would avoid being wasted, which is in accordance with the requirements of green chemistry.

Hongcan Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Pengfei Xia: Formal analysis. Haibao Shao: Investigation. Rong Zhang: Methodology. Changgui Lu: Supervision. Shuhong Xu: Supervision, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Software. Chunlei Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22075043, 21875034, 12274075).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.05.120.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Nano Today
